content:
Both put_user and get_user are for reading predefined size variables from userspace.

Currently in Rust abstractions, there's only UserSlice, which is for arbitrary sizes. I saw it being used in samples/rust/rust_misc_device.rs, in ioctl, even though the variable is just an i32.

However, they are macros. Is it a good idea to make helper functions on the C side to access them?

Boqun Feng said:

See UserSliceReader::read and UserSliceWriter::write.

Yes that's what I meant by UserSlice, or at least generally.

Alice Ryhl said:

What's wrong with UserSlice?

UserSlice uses _copy_from_user and _copy_to_user, which are meant for arrays.
The ones I mentioned, put_user and get_user are faster at copying a single value referencing pointers, like int*. They also don't need a len because, the size is known at compile time.

so the point is that UserSliceReader::read() and UserSliceWriter::write() are for reading/writing with predefined size variables. In theory you can optimize them, but not sure how much value you can get from the optimization.

If you want to do that, you have to:

  • figure out the performance difference between _copy_{from,to}_user() and {put,get}_user().
  • find a place where copy-from/to-user is the hotspot that is worth for optimization.
Rust already skips the bounds checks for UserSlice when the size is known at compile time in Rust.
So I don't think there's any difference

Alice Ryhl said:

Rust already skips the bounds checks for UserSlice when the size is known at compile time in Rust.

But it will still be a for-loop like code on the C side, albeit without bounds checking.

I forgot that, In order to make the variable size known at compile time through a FFI, I can't have void* as a parameter. I need to implement a helper for every variable type, ex:

int rust_helper_put_user_u32(u32 x, u32 __user *to)
{
    return __put_user(x, to);
}
int rust_helper_put_user_u64(u64 x, u64 __user *to)
{
    return __put_user(x, to);
}

Another problem is with __get_user(x, ptr). Because it's a macro, when you pass x to it, it can actually reference and mutate it. So, when implementing a helper function, it should look like this:

int rust_helper_get_user_u32(u32 *x, u32 __user *from)
{
    u32 val;
    int res = __get_user(val, from);
    *x = val;
    return res;
}

Which is more overhead.

Boqun Feng said:

If you want to do that, you have to:

  • figure out the performance difference between _copy_{from,to}_user() and {put,get}_user().
  • find a place where copy-from/to-user is the hotspot that is worth for optimization.

I did a very rough testing using preempt_{disable, enable} and Instant::<Monotonic>::now(). Running each one 10k times in a virtual machine: vng -r arch/x86/boot/bzImage --memory=1G --disable-microvm:

_copy_from_user: 788ns
get_user: 604ns

_copy_to_user: 745ns
put_user: 763ns

I'm not sure if a ~180ns is a significant value in the context of operating systems, but this experiment was insightful none the less.

Thank you.

Thanks for taking a look.

ManeraKai said:

_copy_from_user: 788ns
get_user: 604ns

_copy_to_user: 745ns
put_user: 763ns

Now since _copy_to_user() performs better/similar to put_user(), I'm not sure the performance difference between _copy_from_user() and get_user() comes from the loop (in x86 case it's the rep movsb instruction). How should we explain the difference?

ManeraKai said:

I'm not sure if a ~180ns is a significant value in the context of operating systems, but this experiment was insightful none the less.

You can maybe measure an empty syscall to see whether ~180ns is significant or not, but I'm a bit curious about what contributes to the difference.

Boqun Feng said:

in x86 case it's the rep movsb instruction

I ran gdb on the kernel, and when tracing through _copy_to_user, I found out that my virtual machine wasn't running with FSRM support (Fast Short Rep Mov), so rather than using rep movsb, it used an assembly function called rep_movs_alternative which copies the 4 bytes of my s32 just like a classic for-loop. Yet it was faster than the much simpler put_user that was basically movl :sweat_smile:

I think the benchmarks are deceiving since we are trying to measure a difference between a very small amount of intstructions on a GHz CPU.

In theory, put_user should be faster on CPUs that don't support FSRM.
